figures.caption: Figure 1: Ground-truth potential function ψGT in the FP equation.
Figure 2: Time evolution of the probability density p(x, t) in the FP equation at selected time steps.
Figure 3: Residual-space BIC evaluated along the sequential operator-elimination path for the FP equation.

Table 1: Estimated parameter values for the FP equation. Here, θ̂ denotes the point estimate for VSI and the posterior mean for B-VSI methods. The potential error is computed as MSE(ψ̂) = N−1 g ∑Ng ℓ=1(ψ̂(xℓ) − ψGT(xℓ)) 2 after applying a common additive-constant convention. Variances are reported only for posterior coefficient distributions. Aggregate error metrics are computed from the reported point estimate or posterior mean and are therefore listed without posterior variances.
Figure 4: Covariance matrix of the approximate Gaussian posterior p(θ | D,M∗) for the selected FP model.
Figure 5: Mean and variance of the posterior predictive distribution p(ψ | D,M∗) for the potential function in the FP equation using the approximate Gaussian posterior.
Figure 6: Covariance matrix of the particle posterior p(θ | D,M∗) for the selected FP model estimated using SVGD.
Figure 7: Empirical mean and variance of the posterior predictive distribution p(ψ | D,M∗) for the potential function in the FP equation using SVGD particles.
Figure 8: Ground-truth free-energy density g and partial derivatives ∂g/∂c1 and ∂g/∂c2 used to generate the two-field CH data.
Figure 9: Time evolution of the concentration fields c1 and c2 in the two-field CH equation at selected time steps.
Figure 10: Residual-space BIC evaluated along the sequential operator-elimination path for the two-field CH equation.

Table 2: Estimated effective coefficients for the CH equation. The coefficients ηgiϕ = miθ gi ϕ are mobility-scaled free-energy coefficients, and λi = −miki are signed biharmonic coefficients. Thus, the rows labeled λ1 and λ2 correspond to the PDE coefficients multiplying ∇4c1 and ∇4c2, not to the physical positive coefficients k1 and k2. The notation θ̂ denotes the point estimate for VSI and the posterior mean for B-VSI methods. Variances are reported only for posterior coefficient distributions and for σ2 u when it is inferred. Aggregate error metrics are computed from the reported point estimate or posterior mean and are therefore listed without posterior variances.
Figure 11: Covariance matrix of the approximate Gaussian posterior p(θ | D,M∗) for the selected CH model when the observation-noise variance is known.
Figure 12: Posterior predictive means and variances of the free-energy derivatives in the selected CH model when the observation-noise variance is known.
Figure 13: Covariance matrix of the approximate coefficient posterior for the selected CH model when the scalar observation-noise variance is inferred jointly with the coefficients.
Figure 14: Posterior predictive means and variances of the free-energy derivatives in the selected CH model when the scalar observation-noise variance is inferred jointly with the coefficients.

Figure 15: Sensitivity of parameter-estimation error to observation-noise level and spatial resolution for the FP equation. Each cluster corresponds to a fixed noise level. Within each cluster, the horizontal offsets distinguish different spatial resolutions, with finer grids plotted to the left and coarser grids plotted to the right.

Table 3: Representative computational costs for B-VSI under the block-diagonal residual covariance approximation. Here, N is the number of residual equations per time interval, dθ is the number of active coefficients, d is the dictionary size, and np is the number of SVGD particles. The functions Cfac(N) and Csol(N,m) denote the cost of factoring one residual covariance block and applying its inverse to m right-hand sides, respectively.
